entry.title: Re: Custom Field is automatically uppercase
entry.content:
Dear @kienngo,

The behavior you're observing is driven by the underlying ABAP Dictionary definition of the custom field, not by the value help itself. When a custom field is created in the Custom Fields app with Semantic Type Code List based on CDS View the framework generates the field as CHAR with the domain attribute "Lower Case" not set. This means any value assigned to the field is automatically converted to uppercase at the moment of assignment regardless of how the entry is displayed in the value help dropdown. In your case YY1_PR_RealRequestor is CHAR 95 which is exactly why the selected key is uppercased after picking it from the dropdown

It is worth highlighting that the value help CDS view (e.g. YY1_CustomBusinessUserVH) only supplies the list of entries shown in the dropdown it can return data in any case and the dropdown will display it accordingly. The uppercase conversion happens at the moment the selected key is written into the target CHAR field, which is a property of the target field itself, not of the value help view. Therefore building a new or modified CDS view will not change this behavior

In Public Cloud this is a hard design constraint:

  • The Custom Fields app does not expose any option to toggle the "Lower Case" attribute on a generated CHAR field
  • The Semantic Type of an already published field cannot be changed retroactively

Practical options:

  1. Keep YY1_PR_RealRequestor as the technical key. In Public Edition business user IDs in the user master are themselves uppercase, so the uppercased key still resolves correctly against the user master. What you lose is the visual mixed-case formatting of the embedded display name
  2. Add a complementary custom field of Semantic Type Text, populated via custom logic at save (e.g. BAdI on the Purchasing Document Item). The CHAR field continues to hold the technical key (used for validation, search, integration) and the Text field carries the human-readable, case-preserving label for UI and reporting. This is the standard pattern when both a controlled value and a readable representation are required.
  3. Recreate the field with Semantic Type Text only realistic if the field is not yet productive and contains no data. Note that this removes the CDS-based value help, since Text fields do not support code lists. For PR Real Requestor this is generally not a viable option

More details below:

  • https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2653718
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/key-user-custom-fields-when-to-use-what-type-of-code-list/ba-p/13557853
  • https://me.sap.com/notes/0002792571

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How to display only existing dimension combination on...
entry.content:
Hi Shivam, Thank you so much for your reply!!

I tried something similar on my side, and I can display only the valid rows when the dataset is relatively small. However, when the dataset becomes larger, Excel starts throwing that error message.

I suspect this might be because I am displaying unbooked data, as I want to use the Excel file as a template for users. I did another test where I added a value of 0 to the valid rows and then unchecked “show unbooked data.” With this setup, Excel was able to display the table correctly even when my dataset is large.

That said, I’m not sure whether this is the typical or recommended way to handle this kind of table in SAC, as it feels a bit like a workaround rather than a clean solution...

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to include Freight Charges...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

I asked our internal chatbot for support. Please check whether the response works for you:

To address your requirement in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for handling the ‘Fuel to Carrier’ charge (ZB01) without creating an open item in Accounts Payable, you can consider the following approach:

Solution Approach

  1. Use of Statistical Condition Type: Planned Delivery Cost of Purchasing in S/4HANA Clo… - SAP Community

    • Although you mentioned that making the charge type statistical is not specific per charge type, you can explore configuring the charge type ZB01 as a statistical condition in the pricing procedure. This way, it will not affect the MIRO process directly.

  2. Custom G/L Account Posting: Transportation Management ™ - Posting of Allocated Freight Costs

    • Configure the system to post the ZB01 charge to a specific G/L account that represents fuel consumption. This can be done by setting up a custom account key in the pricing procedure that directs the posting to the desired G/L account instead of the supplier.

  3. Manual Adjustment in MIRO:

    • During the MIRO process, manually adjust the ZB01 line to ensure it does not create a liability. This requires careful handling to ensure the material’s MAP is not adversely affected

Considerations

  • Ensure that any changes made do not disrupt the standard accounting and inventory processes.
  • Test the configuration in a sandbox environment before applying it to the production system to avoid any unintended consequences.

This approach should help you manage the in-kind fuel charge without creating an incorrect liability in the supplier invoice process.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Creating Statutory Reports for Non-Supported Countries in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently working on implementing statutory and legal reporting requirements for a country that is not officially supported by SAP standard localization content in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Our objective is to:

  • Create country-specific statutory reports
  • Adapt report layouts to local legal formats
  • Configure electronic or manual declarations
  • Use available SAP tools such as:
    • Define Statutory Reports
    • Compliance / DRC applications
    • Custom CDS Views
    • SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

We would like to understand the recommended approach and best practices for Public Cloud environments.

Main questions:

  1. What is the standard approach to create statutory reports for non-supported countries in SAP Public Cloud?
  2. Which applications or SSCUIs should be used?
  3. Is it possible to fully customize report queries, mappings, and layouts?
  4. Can custom CDS Views be integrated directly into statutory reporting frameworks?
  5. What are the limitations in Public Cloud compared to Private Cloud / On-Premise?
  6. Has anyone implemented local tax or financial declarations for unsupported countries (example: Morocco)?
  7. Is SAP Document and Reporting Compliance extensible enough for fully custom legal reports?

Any guidance, architecture examples, implementation steps, or lessons learned would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Billing control in Third-Party with Shipping Notification (BD3_GB) – Inbound Delivery dependency
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am currently testing the scope item BD3_GB in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and I have a question regarding billing control.

In this scenario, using item category DB1 (required to enable the billing plan), I have observed that the system allows creating the final billing document (F2) even if the inbound delivery (VL31N) and the goods receipt posting (movement type 101, including GR for account assignment) have not been completed.

From a business perspective, the customer requires that billing should not be allowed if the inbound delivery has not been created or completed.

My questions are the following:

  • Is there any standard configuration that allows blocking billing until the inbound delivery is completed in this scenario?
  • Or is this behavior standard even in processes with shipping notification, meaning that the only possible options would be a manual billing block or custom logic (e.g., via BAdI)?

For example, if we use another item category such as CB1 (without billing plan), when attempting to create the final invoice (F2) without having completed the delivery, the system raises an error and does not allow billing to proceed.

This is exactly the behavior we would like to achieve with item category DB1 when using a billing plan.

 

Thank you very much in advance for your support.

entry.title: SAP Translation Hub Rolls Out Glossary Editor for Intelligent Data Reuse
entry.content:
Managing terminology in SAP Translation Hub used to require working outside the product. To update a glossary entry, you had to export your data to CSV, make your changes in a spreadsheet, and re-import the file, a process that added steps and introduced the risk of formatting errors each time a term needed updating.

That changes today. SAP Translation Hub now includes a glossary editor directly within the Intelligent Data Reuse component, giving you a dedicated UI to manage your terminology without ever leaving the product.

What's new?
The glossary editor lets you work with your terminology data in place:

Search and filter: Quickly locate the entries you need across your glossary, even as it grows.
Edit entries: Update source terms, target terms, or definitions directly in the UI, no CSV required.
Delete entries: Remove outdated or incorrect terms without touching a file.

What does this mean for your workflow?

The previous approach of exporting, editing, and re-importing a CSV file added unnecessary steps every time a term needed updating, and with manual file handling comes the risk of formatting errors, accidental overwrites, or data loss on re-import. The new editor eliminates these failure points entirely.

How to get started
The glossary editor is available now in the Intelligent Data Reuse component of SAP Translation Hub on SAP BTP (regions EU10, EU11, and US10). To access it:

1. Open SAP Translation Hub and navigate to Intelligent Data Reuse
2. Select Manage Glossaries in the left pane
3. Open a glossary to start searching, editing, or deleting entries

 

Learn More
SAP Translation Hub Documentation
Intelligent Data Reuse, Working with Glossaries
Contact us: translationhub@sap.com

Have questions about the glossary editor or how to get the most out of term-aware translation? Comment below or reach out to us directly.

Hello @FabianSCC,

my understanding is that as of today, there is currently no processor available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. However, I do believe SAP is planning to provide a successor.

 

My answer is based on the following documentation: Invoice Management Reporting | SAP Help Portal

  • Here you can find the Invoice Management Reporting Process and its components.
  • Display Reprocessing Rate of Incoming Payments is listed as a process step.

 

However, when you click on "Display Reprocessing Rate of Incoming Payments", there is no specific app linked:

 

In comparison: all other process steps contain a link to a specific app, like here:

 

I would suggest to do the following:

  • Set this Help Portal Entry as a favourite → you should then get a notification if it gets updated.
  • Create an improvement request on Homepage - Customer Influence and demand the release of an app for Public Cloud similar to F1619 Reprocessing Rate of Incoming Payments (which is available in Private Cloud). 

 

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: SAP Logistics Management: How to Integrate with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Modern supply chains demand flexibility, transparency, and seamless execution across warehouse and transportation processes. With SAP Logistics Management, SAP introduces a cloud-native solution designed to meet exactly these needs—covering logistics processes end to end while integrating tightly with your core ERP landscape.

But SAP Logistics Management doesn’t operate in isolation. Its true value is unlocked through native integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, enabling organizations to extend their logistics capabilities without disrupting existing ERP processes.

How the Integration Works

Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is a central design principle of SAP Logistics Management.

Beyond ERP integration, SAP Logistics Management also connects seamlessly with:

  • SAP Business Network for Logistics, enabling collaboration with carriers, warehouse operators, and logistics service providers
  • Third-party logistics providers, allowing companies to extend their logistics ecosystem beyond SAP-managed environments

This integrated approach ensures that logistics processes remain synchronized across internal systems and external partners, reducing manual effort and improving execution quality.

Learn It Live: Integration and Configuration in Practice

If you want to understand how to integrate SAP Logistics Management, don’t miss the upcoming live session on June 3rd.

In this session, SAP experts will walk you through:

  • How SAP Logistics Management is integrated with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • How to configure integration with SAP Business Network for Logistics and third-party providers

Register Now

You can find full details and register here:

👉 Live Session

If you're looking to modernize your logistics processes while staying fully integrated with your SAP digital core, this session is one you won’t want to miss.

entry.title: SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info - May Edition
entry.content:
This blog edition is intended to provide updates on "hot" topics in relation to legal changes affecting the SAP Global Trade Services products in the month of May.  For previous editions please see the Past Editions section at the end of the blog.

This is not a replacement for the official notes released or the the Regulatory Change Manager announcements but rather supplementary information.  These announcements are also available in KBA  3707712 - SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info!!

11.05.2026
UK NTCS 6: Introduction of NCTS Phase 6 for transit shipments in the United Kingdom.
The communication with the United Kingdom's customs authorities (HMRC) for the transit procedure  is to be switched to NCTS phase 6. The Regulatory Change Manager has now been updated with the delivery of  SAP Note(3745794) in relation to the changes.

  • The legal change is mandatory for users of the NCTS system.
  • The proposed effective date is on June 1, 2026.
  • A trader test environment is expected to be available from April 2026.
  • This information is based on the information available at the time of publication of this blog. 

DE EMCS 2.7 for administrative documents in Germany

Communication with the German customs authorities is to be switched from EMCS 2.5 to EMCS 2.7.
The Regulatory Change Manager has been updated with the solving SAP Note(3715370)

Additionally you can find the rollout material Germany_EMCS_2.7_Documentation_Version_1.0.pdf attached to SAP Note(3715370)
The Version_1.0.  of the roll out material contains information on the following:

 

Past editions of this blog:

SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info - April Edition -  (CH PASSAR NE015 V5, NL DMS: Digitaal Dossier)
SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info

entry.title: Re: Clarification on PO Reference Handling in JournalEntr...
entry.content:
Dear @elmntx,

There is no standard API workaround. The PO linkage (EBELN/EBELP on the special G/L item + PO history update in EKBE) is only achievable via the Fiori app, not via the Journal Entry Post API. SAP confirms this directly in KBA 3479539 the API supports posting customer/supplier down payment with the Special G/L indicator in DownPaymentTerms, however connecting the down payment to a down payment request is not supported yet this should be a new development request. SAP points customers to Customer Influence (KBA 2963059) as the only structural path

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3479539

https://me.sap.com/notes/2963059

What can realistically be done:

  • True PO link (EKBE, Monitor PO Down Payments, automatic clearing against the supplier invoice) only via Manage Supplier Down Payment Requests (F1688) or Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments. No API equivalent. As a last resort, RPA through SAP Build Process Automation can drive this app end-to-end
  • Informational reference only via the API (NO PO history update) the PO number can be passed in standard header fields of the Journal Entry Post API (e.g. DocumentHeaderText) or via a custom field on a supported business context but this is reporting/traceability only. The standard down payment clearing logic and the Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments app rely on PO history (EKBE) and the native PO fields on the special G/L item, so they will not pick up the link

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3585066

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/guidelines-for-api-journal-entry-post/ba-p/13421397

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Portable Local SAML Assertion Generator for SuccessFactors APIs (No Installations required etc.)
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

Here is another way to gerenerate a signed SAML 2.0 bearer assertion locally without any installation for testing SFAPIs.

What It Is

A portable Windows GUI. Unzip, double-click SAMLAssertionGen.bat, done. Python runtime is bundled so there is nothing to install. Language refinement and code development done with the help of AI.

Same assertion logic as the Java original. Same XML structure, same RSA-SHA256 signature, same www.successfactors.com issuer and audience. I didn't reinvent anything, just made it easier to run.

Fill in the fields. Click Generate. Copy to clipboard.

You may save your own SAMLAssertion.properties file so values load automatically or just fill up the GUI.

Setup

1. Unzip anywhere.
2. Edit SAMLAssertion.properties:
 
  • tokenUrl - SF OAuth token endpoint
  • clientId - SF API key
  • userId - SF API user
  • userName - fallback if userId is empty
  • privateKey — Base64-encoded PKCS#8 RSA key downloaded from SF - Manage OAuth2 Client Applications
  • expireInMinutes — default 10, keep it short
 
3. Run SAMLAssertionGen.bat.
4. Click Generate Assertion, then Copy to Clipboard.

 

Using the Assertion

POST {tokenUrl} Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

company_id={companyId}&client_id={clientId}&grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:saml2-bearer&assertion={your Base64 assertion}

In Postman: Body > raw > paste the form string above. The response returns a Bearer token. Add it as an Authorization header on any subsequent API call.

 

 

 

 

Technical Notes

XML mirrors the Java original Issuer and Audience set to www.successfactors.com, NotBefore backdated 10 minutes for clock skew, enveloped XML DSIG with RSA-SHA256. Libraries: lxml, signxml, cryptography — all bundled.

Security

  • Never commit your properties file. It contains your private key.
  • Treat the assertion like a password. It grants API access until expiry.
  • Keep expiration short. 10 minutes is the right default.

Download and extract the ZIP file:

https://github.com/wes-teros14/PortableSAMLAssertionGen

 

Conclusion

This utility simplifies the process of generating signed SAML assertions for SuccessFactors API testing by removing installation steps and packaging everything into a portable executable setup.

entry.title: Product Bulk Upload Fails for Product Part Numbers When Leading Zeros Are Removed by Excel
entry.content:
During the product bulk upload, the system currently requires part numbers to include leading zeros in order to be processed correctly (in case product part numbers have leading zeros under Setup).
This behavior is not user-friendly and leads to inconsistencies, as Excel typically removes leading zeros automatically when part numbers with leading 0s are added.
And when we copy the part numbers from the Excel into the bulk add option in quotes, we get error "product is not found".
The expected behavior is that the system should accept part numbers regardless of whether leading zeros are included or not.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Product Bulk Upload Fails for Product Part Numbers Wh...
entry.content:
The current behavior is expected because Microsoft Excel automatically treats values consisting only of digits as numeric values and removes any leading zeros.

To avoid this issue, please maintain the part number column in Excel as Text/String format instead of Number format before entering or pasting the values.

Please format the Excel column as Text before adding the part numbers.

Microsoft documentation regarding preserving leading zeros in Excel:

  • Keeping leading zeros and large numbers - Microsoft Support
  • Format numbers to keep leading zeros in Excel for the web - Microsoft Support


Once the values are maintained as text, the bulk upload and bulk add functionality should work correctly.

entry.title: C_TS4FI_2601 System-Based Assessment – Possible Scoring Discrepancy (Multiple Candidates Same Score)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,
I recently took the C_TS4FI_2601 (SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Financial Accounting) System-Based Assessment and received a score of 52.94% against a cut score of 60%, with status marked as Failed.
I want to flag a concern — multiple candidates from our batch received the exact same 52.94% score, which raises doubts about whether the AI scoring algorithm evaluated our responses correctly.
I noticed a similar issue was already reported here on the community where a candidate scored 68% but was marked Failed (Feb 2026). This suggests there may be broader scoring issues with the new System-Based Assessment format.
My questions:
1. Is there a known issue with the C_TS4FI_2601 scoring engine?
2. How do I formally request a score review or raise a support ticket?
3. Has anyone else experienced identical scores across multiple candidates?
I have already screenshotted my result showing exam ID C_TS4FI_2601 and the 52.94% score.
Any guidance from SAP or the community would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you.

entry.title: Cloud ALM - resources performance - Job & Automation Monitoring and Health Monitoring
entry.content:
1. Once the settings are enabled (in Job & Automation Monitoring" and " Health Monitoring" ) will this affect S4 RFC resources and performance? How should the freruency be adjusted?
  2. Is the job execution a one-time collection or a daily collection?
  3. How should alerting auto closed

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Cloud ALM - resources performance - Job & Automation...
entry.content:
Dear Customer,

Thank you for your questions. Please find the clarifications below:

 

1. Impact on S/4 RFC resources and performance

Enabling Job & Automation Monitoring and Health Monitoring has minimal impact on system resources.

For ABAP (on-premise) systems, data collection is based on ST-PI, and RFC resources are generally not significantly affected. It is recommended to keep the latest Cloud ALM correction notes implemented to ensure optimal performance (see KBA 0003247014).

Monitoring collects technical and job-related metadata only (not business data) using a lightweight approach, with periodic or event-driven collection. Overall, RFC load remains low and controlled, and no performance degradation is expected.

 

2. Frequency and data collection behaviour

Monitoring is continuous, not one-time or daily-only:

  • Job & Automation Monitoring: captures each job execution (start, end, status, runtime) in near real-time
  • Health Monitoring: collects technical metrics (e.g., CPU, memory) at regular intervals

Data collection is typically:

  • Event-driven (for jobs), and
  • Interval-based (minutes-level, for metrics)

As per SAP guidance, it is recommended to use the standard collection interval. Only for specific requirements should the interval be adjusted (see KBA 0003247014).

 

3. Alert auto-closure behaviour

Alerts are handled as follows:

  • Automatically close when the underlying issue is resolved (status returns to green)
  • Can also be manually closed/confirmed
  • There is no time-based (age-based) auto-closure

More details are available here: Alerting | SAP Help Portal 

Best Regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: SAP Joule in Action: Redefining Business Intelligence
entry.content:
Introduction

The evolution of enterprise systems is entering a new phase one where AI is intergarted and actively  collaborates with users. With the introduction of SAP Joule, SAP has positioned itself at the forefront of this transformation by delivering a generative AI copilot across business applications.

SAP Joule AI represents a paradigm shift in how users interact with SAP systems such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors.

working on AI transformations, I have observed a clear shift: business users are no longer satisfied with transactional systems they expect intelligent assistants embedded directly into workflows. Joule aligns exactly with this expectation, bridging the gap between business intent and system execution.

What is SAP Joule?

SAP Joule is a generative AI copilot and more embedded across SAP’s cloud portfolio. It enables users to:

  • Interact with SAP systems using natural language
  • Automate complex workflows
  • Generate insights in real time
  • Enhance decision-making with contextual intelligence

 

Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform, Joule leverages AI models integrated with enterprise data while ensuring governance, security, and compliance.

From my implementation experience, one of the biggest challenge in SAP systems has been user navigation complexity. Joule’s conversational interface eliminates the need for deep transaction code knowledge, which is particularly valuable in large-scale deployments involving non-technical users.

Why SAP Joule Matters

Traditional ERP systems are transactional. SAP Joule makes them conversational and intelligent.

 

Key Value Drivers:

  • Productivity Boost: Reduce manual effort through AI-assisted automation
  • User Experience Transformation: Shift from UI-driven to intent-driven interactions
  • Faster Decision-Making: Real-time contextual insights
  • Process Optimization: Intelligent recommendations embedded in workflows

During SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, especially in success factor / supply chain environments, decision-making often depends on multiple reports and manual analysis. Joule has the potential to compress decision cycles from hours to minutes by generating real-time insights directly within the process

SAP Joule Architecture Overview

 

 

A typical Joule-enabled landscape includes:

Core ERP Layer

  • SAP S/4HANA (Digital Core)

AI & Extension Layer

  • SAP BTP (AI services, integration, extensions)

Data Layer

  • SAP Datasphere / SAP HANA Cloud

AI Copilot Layer

  • SAP Joule embedded in Fiori applications

Integration Layer

  • APIs, OData services, Event Mesh

 In S/4HANA  implementations , maintaining a clean core is critical. Joule fits well into this paradigm because it leverages BTP for AI processing, avoiding heavy customizations in the core ERP system.

Implementation Approach for SAP Joule

Phase 1: Readiness Assessment

  • Evaluate system landscape (ECC vs S/4HANA)
  • Identify high-value AI use cases
  • Assess data quality and governance maturity

 

Data readiness is the single biggest success factor. Without harmonized master data and clean transactional data, AI outputs , no matter how advanced , lose business relevance.

Phase 2: Platform Enablement

  • Activate SAP BTP services
  • Configure AI capabilities
  • Ensure connectivity with S/4HANA

Architectural side treat BTP not just as a platform, but as an AI innovation layer. Joule implementation makes it central to the architecture.

Phase 3: Use Case Identification

Common SAP Joule use cases:

Supply Chain

  • Demand forecasting insights
  • Exception handling automation

Procurement

  • Smart supplier recommendations
  • Contract summarization

HR

  • Job description generation
  • Leave insights

 

I see immediate value in navigation  where Joule can proactively suggest actions instead of users searching to find the panel.

Phase 4: Integration & Extension

  • Extend Joule capabilities using SAP BTP
  • Integrate with external AI models if required
  • Enable APIs for custom workflows

In complex enterprise landscapes (e.g., SAP + external logistics platforms), Joule can act as a unified intelligence layer, reducing dependency on multiple dashboards and tools.

Phase 5: Security & Governance

  • Role-based access control
  • Data privacy compliance
  • AI explainability and auditability

From an enterprise architecture standpoint, I see AI governance is not optional. Organizations must define:

  • What data Joule can access
  • How decisions are audited
  • How AI outputs are validated

This has become especially critical in regulated industries.

Phase 6: Change Management

  • User training for conversational AI
  • Adoption strategy
  • Continuous feedback loop

One underestimated challenge is user trust in AI. Adoption improves significantly when users understand:

  • How Joule generates responses
  • When to trust vs validate outputs

Best Practices for SAP Joule Implementation

  • Start with Business Value, Not Technology
  • Prioritize Clean Core in S/4HANA
  • Leverage Standard SAP AI Capabilities First
  • Ensure High Data Quality
  • Adopt Agile AI Deployment Approach

We Started with assistive AI (recommendations) before moving to autonomous AI (decision-making). This phased approach reduces risk and improves adoption.

Challenges to Consider

  • Data readiness and harmonization
  • User adoption resistance
  • AI governance and compliance
  • Integration complexity in hybrid landscapes

In real projects, the biggest blocker is not technology it’s organizational readiness. AI transformation requires alignment across IT, business, and leadership.

Future Outlook

SAP Joule will evolve into a central AI orchestrator, enabling:

  • Autonomous business processes
  • AI-driven decision intelligence
  • Multi-agent enterprise ecosystems

Given my work in Agentic AI, I see Joule as a foundation for multi-agent enterprise systems, where different AI agents collaborate across supply chain, finance, and operations.

Conclusion

SAP Joule represents a shift from transactional ERP systems to intelligent, AI-driven enterprises.

For SAP architects and leaders, the focus should be on aligning AI capabilities with business outcomes, ensuring governance, and building scalable AI-driven ecosystems.

From an SAP architect’s strategic perspective, Joule is not just another feature it is a strategic capability that will redefine how users interact with enterprise systems. Organizations that successfully adopt it will gain a significant competitive advantage in automation, decision-making, and operational efficiency.

entry.title: Is SAP CPE the Missing Answer to Geopolitical Raw Material Price Volatility?
entry.content:
Raw material prices do not wait for purchase orders to close. That gap between when a PO is raised and when goods actually arrive has always created friction between Procurement and Finance but in today's environment, with commodity markets reacting to sanctions, shipping disruptions, and currency swings, the gap has become expensive.

This article documents personal technical research into SAP Commodity Pricing Engine (CPE) and what it actually offers organizations running S/4HANA in commodity-intensive environments. The exposure here is research and architecture-based rather than live implementation, but the process logic is worth walking through in detail.

The Operational Problem

The core issue is timing.

A Purchase Order gets created at today's market rate. Goods Receipt happens three weeks later. The supplier invoice arrives two weeks after that. By then, the LME copper price or Platts crude benchmark has moved, and none of the original figures hold.

What typically fills that gap in practice:

  • Manual invoice adjustments processed outside the system
  • Inventory valuation entries that do not reflect actual procurement cost
  • Back-and-forth reconciliation between AP and Procurement
  • Period-end corrections that obscure the true financial effect of price movement

The organization ends up managing market volatility through human effort rather than system logic. CPE exists to change that.

How CPE Structures the Pricing Lifecycle

 

CPE replaces static price conditions with formula-based pricing that moves through defined stages across the procurement cycle.

Stage 1  Provisional Pricing at PO Creation

When the PO is created, the system applies a provisional value while leaving the pricing condition open for future recalculation. From a finance perspective, this is the right approach. It allows the company to recognize a procurement commitment without locking an unreliable price into inventory valuation at the earliest and least certain point in the process.

Stage 2  Revaluation During Goods Receipt

During MIGO posting, the system can recalculate pricing based on quotation dates and market reference logic tied to the actual delivery timeline. In environments where commodity prices shift daily, this means inventory is valued at a price that reflects when the goods actually arrived not when the order was raised.

The standard integration supporting this revaluation is more mature than commonly assumed, and does not require the level of custom development that many consultants would initially expect.

Stage 3  Differential Invoicing in MIRO

The staged structure provisional, differential, final settlement means that pricing differences are processed through defined system steps rather than landing as unplanned corrections in Accounts Payable.

In high-volume procurement environments, this matters operationally. AP teams spend real time chasing and correcting invoice variances. A structured differential invoicing process reduces that workload considerably.

The Material Ledger Connection

The most technically significant part of this research was understanding how CPE interacts with Material Ledger and Actual Costing.

Even when procurement prices are changing throughout the period, the resulting variances still flow into Material Ledger settlement during period-end closing  CKMLCP runs, cost component splits, multilevel settlement. The commodity volatility does not disappear into manual adjustments. It shows up as actual cost data that management can analyze.

That integration means CPE is not only a procurement pricing tool. From a FICO perspective, it functions as a financial control mechanism that keeps valuation accuracy intact across a procurement cycle that spans multiple weeks and multiple price points.

What Realistic Implementation Requires

CPE is not a feature that gets switched on during a standard S/4HANA activation. The complexity is real.

Successful implementation depends on several areas working together:

  • Pricing formula design that accurately maps to actual business procurement scenarios
  • Clear ownership of quotation management and market data governance in operations
  • Reliable integration with external quotation providers such as LME or Platts
  • Activation of the relevant business functions, including LOG_CPE_SD_MM
  • Sustained collaboration between Procurement, Finance, and IT throughout the project

One area that remains open for further investigation is how organizations govern quotation data in productive environments  specifically who owns the market reference data, how it is validated, and how discrepancies are handled when external feeds are delayed or corrected.

That operational dimension may explain why CPE remains underutilized relative to what the standard functionality can actually deliver.

Closing Perspective

The strongest argument for CPE is not the pricing mechanics themselves. It is that the entire approach is built around connecting procurement execution directly to financial valuation through standard processes rather than custom workarounds built on top of a system that was not designed for them.

For organizations operating in commodity-driven markets, that connection has real financial value. The question is whether implementation programs are structured to support it properly.

Perspectives from consultants with live CPE experience are genuinely welcome:

  • Where did the configuration complexity actually surface in practice?
  • How did the Material Ledger integration hold up during real period-end closing?
  • Is CPE still being passed over inside S/4HANA programs that would clearly benefit from it?

References

  1. SAP Help Portal – Commodity Pricing Engine (CPE) https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/8308e6d301d54584a33cd04a9861bc52/311e58a9317c4341a1f9d43ba9fc9349.html

  2. SAP Help Portal – Logistics, Commodity Pricing Engine in SD and MM https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/77c07c8d30664260a0b3ff864e6b5e78/7f72f9a33cbb4d01bcccced9ab40a7bf.html

  3. SAP Help Portal – Material Ledger and Actual Costing https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/56f7319a9048445eb86221af73cab72b/bcd6cc5340487214e10000000a174cb4.html

  4. SAP – Commodity Management for Finance and Procurement https://www.sap.com/products/scm/commodity-management.html

The technical observations, process analysis, and conclusions in this article are entirely the author's own, developed through direct review of SAP documentation and architectural research. AI writing tools were used selectively to support image creation, sentence clarity and article structure not to generate the technical content itself.

Mohammed Abood

Senior SAP FICO Consultant

entry.title: Collection of SAP Innovation Award 2026 Public Services Entries
entry.content:
Congratulations to all public services customers and partners who submitted entries this year. Your commitment to leveraging SAP solutions to improve lives, strengthen governance, and drive operational excellence continues to inspire us all.

This year’s submissions demonstrated remarkable ingenuity from AI-powered infrastructure decisions to real-time financial intelligence platforms serving millions of citizens. We are proud to spotlight the exceptional organizations leading the way.

 

Award Winners

City of Madrid (Spain) - Winner of the Industry Leader category

Madrid's vision for an AI-driven, citizen-centric capital was realized by replacing fragmented legacy systems with a unified SAP cloud foundation. This transformation eliminated manual bottlenecks, enabled real-time controls and AI-assisted audits, and achieved significant outcomes: over 10,000 workdays saved annually, consolidation of 12 million taxpayer records into 5.1 million verified golden records and 98% property tax collection efficiency. Beyond these metrics, the project restored legal certainty and public trust, accelerated citizen services, and established a scalable, clean-core platform that allows the city to rapidly deploy new digital and AI capabilities. This transformation not only benefits Madrid's 3.5 million residents but also sets a new standard for modern public administration.

Ministry of Defence of Ukraine - Winner of the Financial Futurist category

The Ministry of Defence of Ukraine replaced slow, spreadsheet-driven budget routines with a secure cloud-based financial intelligence platform that consolidated 2.8 million treasury documents, governed $65.6 billion across 800+ spending units, and real-time financial visibility. The system cut reporting labor by roughly 67%, eliminated paper processes, improved audit traceability and governance, and enabled faster, more accurate resource allocation while establishing an AI-ready foundation for predictive risk management. By turning delayed summaries into live, drill‑down financial command, the system strengthened decision quality and national resilience when every hour matters.

Federal Tax Authority (UAE) - Winner of the Services Superstar category

The Federal Tax Authority scaled its EmaraTax ecosystem on SAP Tax and Revenue Management to support the new regime, delivering a single, resilient national tax platform that supports end-to-end registration, filing, payments and audit-ready accounting. The solution handled processed about one million filings during busy periods and achieving a 92%+ filing rate while sustaining uninterrupted service and high customer satisfaction. Built for rapid, configuration-driven change, EmaraTax preserved public trust, reinforced compliance and positioned the UAE with a future-ready, scalable tax administration that won the Zero-Bureaucracy award.

 

Finalist

Department of Transport and Main Roads (Queensland, Australia) - Finalist in the AI Excellence category

Queensland’s Department of Transport and Main Roads transformed its pavement planning system by implementing ADVICE, a cloud-based, AI-enabled optimization platform utilizing SAP AI Core. This new system models whole-of-life costs and millions of treatment scenarios across 33,000 km of roads over a 20-year horizon. Engineers can now run statewide optimizations in just one day, instead of a week, freeing approximately $9.5 million annually for reinvestment in high-priority maintenance. This change boosts safety, extends asset life, and ensures every dollar of the $500 million annual pavement program delivers maximum public value.

Hacienda Bogotá (Colombia) - Finalist in the Industry Leader category

Bogotá's Secretariat of Treasury transformed its financial systems by deploying SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Tax and Revenue Management, and a secure Oficina Virtual that authenticates taxpayers with national registries, removing identity-driven revenue leakage and creating a unified source of truth for budget, treasury, and accounting. This transformation yielded substantial outcomes, with $3.7 billion collected in 2024, $370 million recovered in overdue debt, and process times reduced from 15 days to 1. This transformation reinstated public trust and legal certainty, enabled efficient budget management, funded critical infrastructure and social programs, and serves as a repeatable model for district-wide treasury modernization.

 

Champions of Innovation

Beyond the winners and finalists, we are proud to recognize the following organizations whose projects exemplify the transformative potential of SAP solutions in the public sector:

OrganizationProject Highlight
Services AustraliaBuilding a once-in-a-generation aged care technology transformation with SAP S/4HANA to process nearly 4 million invoices and enabling over 352,000 care recipients to receive timely, dignified care.
ALTODeployed SAP S/4HANA Cloud in just six months, achieving full operational independence on time, on scope, and on budget, with zero critical issues, establishing a replicable model for rapid, clean-core cloud transformation in the public sector.
Junta de Castilla y León
Achieving intelligent hyper-automation in the public sector with SAP Build Process Automation and SAP BRF+, leading to elimination of 400,000 repetitive tasks within six months and reduced contract processing time from 30 days to just 3, significantly enhancing efficiency, flexibility, and consistency in procurement processes.
Ministry of State for Foreign AffairsReplaced manual, paper-based decree management process with a fully digital, end-to-end solution built on SAP BTP to automate decree generation, approvals, and system updates, reducing approval times by 98% from 3-4 months to just 1-2 days, while delivering full transparency, compliance, and audit readiness across its global diplomatic operations.
California Department of Corrections and RehabilitationModernized aging ERP infrastructure by migrating to SAP S/4HANA Cloud in a FedRAMP-compliant environment, delivering a 30% improvement in reporting performance, transforming siloed, manual operations into a secure, real-time, and future-ready platform.
City of TacomaDeploy an AI-enabled automated conversion tool during its SAP S/4HANA transformation, reducing manual development effort by 30–50% and accelerating time-to-analytics by 20%, delivering faster, more reliable insights that directly improve utility services and community outcomes for nearly 220,000 residents.
Bradesco FoundationReplaced decades-old decentralized systems with SAP S/4HANA Cloud implementation across 40 schools, cutting payment cycles from 25 to 6 days and eliminating 1,500 hours of manual work monthly, redirecting every efficiency gain back into free, high-quality education for 42,000 students.
Singapore Management UniversityReplaced fragmented, manual finance and grant management operations with SAP S/4HANA Cloud implementation, processing 2,755 POs, 1,333 payments, and 325 AR collections within the first month, delivering real-time grant visibility, embedded budget controls, and a scalable digital finance backbone.
Singapore Management UniversityUnified fragmented procurement and travel operations by implementing SAP Ariba and SAP Concur, processing 83% of expense claims automatically and delivering real-time spend visibility and policy-driven governance.
Singapore Management UniversityUnified its fragmented HR operations across 2,500 staff with SAP SuccessFactors, triggering 3,889 automated workflows and saving over 1,000 minutes of manual effort within the first three weeks, shifting HR from a gatekeeper to a strategic enabler.
Medical College of Virginia Foundation
Advancing academic medicine with SAP Cloud ERP has led to a 99.8% reduction in fund balance query times and a 99% decrease in report generation time, enabling real-time visibility across a nearly billion-dollar endowment, ensuring faster and more reliable distribution of critical funding for scholarships, research, and patient care.
Defense Logistics Agency
Delivered a mission-scale warehouse modernization with SAP EWM while maintaining mission operations and established a repeatable modernization blueprint that enhances warfighter readiness and strengthens confidence across DLA’s network of over 90+ distribution sites.

 

A special thank you to Ryan Van Leent for his outstanding leadership in championing the Innovation Awards for public services customers. Check out his thoughtful recognition of our customers’ achievements through his LinkedIn post. His dedication to elevating public sector innovation within the SAP ecosystem continues to make a meaningful difference.

We also extend our sincere gratitude to the SAP colleagues, partners, and customers who invested their time, expertise, and passion into crafting and supporting these submissions. Your contributions make recognition like this possible.

Most importantly, thank you to all our public services customers for your continued trust in SAP and your unwavering commitment to using technology as a force for public good. Your stories inspire governments and institutions around the world to reimagine what is possible.

We look forward to seeing what you will achieve next.

Interested in learning more about how SAP supports public sector transformation? Explore our SAP Community Page for Public Sector.

entry.title: Agent-led transformation with RISE with SAP Methodology
entry.content:
The world is moving on: The shift from traditional ERP to Agentic ERP is becoming the major trend across enterprises.

» 69% of organizations plan to modernize their ERP system today
(source: IDC's Worldwide Application Services Survey, January 2025).

» 70% are investing in intelligent ERP systems to enhance agility, speed, and configurability
(source: IDC's SaaSPath Survey, May 2025).

» 66% are switching their ERP systems for embedded AI workflows and higher ROI.

The challenge for aging ERP systems

Many organizations running aging ERP systems struggle to realize a return on investment from custom-built or third-party AI in legacy landscapes. Their aging system landscapes increasingly hold them back from keeping pace with the rising demand from customers and markets for innovative processes. Will they be successful with Agentic ERP? Yes, if they modernize their ERP core, govern AI agents rigorously, and scale use cases that deliver measurable value.

From initial AI models to AI assistants

The transition from initial AI models to AI assistants and finally to agentic orchestrators represents a shift from "chatting" to "doing." While many current AI models were primarily reactive engines for generating text or code, AI assistants evolved to act as personalized interfaces that could use tools under direct human supervision. In 2026, the frontier moved toward multi-agent orchestration, where a primary "assistant" (or supervisor) no longer just answers questions but actively delegates complex workflows to specialized agents. These agents act as domain experts, such as "System Analysis”, “Data Management” or “Project Management”, allowing AI to reason, plan, and execute multi-step goals autonomously while the human user shifts from a "doer" to a "reviewer."

In practice, that means AI assistants not only help transform the business, but also help run it, continuously optimizing operations while freeing people to focus on higher value work.

At SAPPHIRE 2026, SAP’s vision for the Autonomous Enterprise placed AI assistants and agents at the center, not only to optimize day-to-day operations, but also to accelerate and derisk the transformation to the Cloud building on an agile, resilient clean core.

Agent-led transformation

While customers value the RISE with SAP Methodology and its toolchain for guiding and streamlining transformation, they consistently voice the same concerns starting a transformation project: how to manage risk and change, and how to reduce or optimize resource intensive work such as system landscape analysis, custom code and data migration, quality assurance and testing, or user adoption and rollout management.
To address these concerns, SAP introduced the "agent-led toolchain" as part of the RISE with SAP Methodology. This approach distinguishes itself by accelerating ERP modernization, significantly reducing time-consuming activities, and eliminating complexity thereby facilitating a value-driven business transformation.

Capabilities for ERP migration
Support customers by reducing time-consuming key activities:

System and landscape analysis – connect and analysis the as-is situation

Project Management – agent-led collaboration across activities

Custom code migration – discovery, optimization and migration by reducing technical debt in line with clean core principles

Data migration - analysis, cleaning, preparation and migration to improve quality and speed

Testing, validation, quality and change management to reduce disruption

Configuration agent - proactive guidance for best practices settings – autonomous if permitted

System rollout across multiple geographies, managing localization requirements

The RISE with SAP Methodology: A catalyst for change

The methodology uniquely combines three components to support the journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise, further enhanced by Joule and AI.

  • Standardized Framework with best practice project roadmap templates, helping customers crafting a project roadmap tailored to their specific needs. 
  • Expert guidance empowered by AI enhanced by SAP and partners human intelligence.   
    From discover and onboard, to clean core principles, project governance, and continuous innovation.
  • Agent-led toolchain for a streamlined collaboration and efficient project execution to ensure a cohesive, efficient workflow throughout people, process, applications and data.

Instead of working with separate guidelines and tools, SAP provides a unified approach helping to manage change holistically.  As a result, you can transition more smoothly and handle complex transformation processes with greater clarity, improving operational efficiency, and achieving your business goals. You also gain the flexibility to adapt and innovate continuously, in line with SAP’s clean core principles.
For more information see Link

Success Stories

Many organizations have already embarked on this transformational journey with notable success. Companies have reported enhanced process efficiencies, improved operational agility, and accelerated digital transformation timelines. By empowering agents and leveraging the strength of the RISE with SAP Methodology and the agent-led toolchain businesses are not only surviving but thriving in the digital age.

Conclusion: The future is agent-led

As organizations strive to navigate the complexities of the modern business environment, agent-led transformation with the RISE with SAP Methodology emerges as a powerful strategy. By equipping assistants with the right tools and insights, businesses can accelerate transformation, drive innovation, and achieve sustainable growth. Embracing this approach means embracing a future of agility, resilience, and ongoing success.

Are you ready to engage your project team, empower your agents, and transform your business?

Explore how the RISE with SAP Methodology and its comprehensive agent-let toolchain can catalyze your organization’s journey to becoming an Autonomous Enterprise.

visit SAP.com RISE with SAP Methodology

entry.title: 3 takeaways from our OMR discussion on AI & CX 💡
entry.content:
OMR 2026 was an incredible experience for SAP Women in Tech 

Together with the Business Women Network Hamburg, we hosted an inspiring discussion around AI and Customer Experience featuring Andre Bechtold and Michelle Hildenbrand (SAP), Evangelia Mitoli (Douglas Marketing Solutions), and Ariane Bensch (Microsoft).

My personal key learnings from the discussion:
- AI orchestrates customer journeys in real time. But the strategy behind it still comes from humans.

- B2B or B2C — people remain at the center. No matter the business model, customers always come first.

- Emotions are still the most powerful CX tool. Customers may forget features, but they remember how you made them feel. 

What was your biggest AI takeaway or trend from OMR this year?

entry.title: SAP Joule in Asset Management: Embedded AI Capabilities in S/4HANA 2025
entry.content:
Overview

With S/4HANA 2025 Private Edition, SAP embeds generative AI into Asset Management through SAP Joule, enabling natural‑language interaction across standard SAP Fiori applications. Joule allows maintenance engineers, planners and technicians to retrieve information and execute transactions conversationally while preserving governance and process integrity.

This blog highlights key embedded Joule capabilities for Asset Management, illustrated with real example prompts and screenshots from S/4HANA 2025.

1.      Display Maintenance Orders and Notifications

Joule allows maintenance users to quickly access execution data using natural language prompts.

1.1 Display Maintenance Notifications

Users can retrieve maintenance notifications by specifying equipment or technical objects.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance notifications of technical object 210100091

SAP Joule displaying maintenance notifications using a natural‑language prompt in Find Maintenance Notification (F2071).

1.2 Display Maintenance Orders

Maintenance orders can be retrieved in a similar way.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders of technical object 210100004

Maintenance orders retrieved via SAP Joule and displayed in Find Maintenance Orders (F2175).

2.      Perform Maintenance Jobs

One of the strongest Joule capabilities is support for maintenance execution, integrated with
F5104A – Perform Maintenance Jobs.

2.1 View Maintenance Jobs

Users can retrieve job lists based on ownership, dates, work centre, or maintenance order.

Example prompt

  • Show my jobs of this week

Maintenance jobs displayed using SAP Joule in Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A).

2.2 View Jobs in Calendar

Jobs can also be visualised in a calendar view.

Example prompt

  • Display my jobs in calendar

Calendar view of maintenance jobs generated via SAP Joule in Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A).

Example prompt

  • Display all maintenance job having technical object 210100091 in calendar

Display maintenance jobs for a specific technical object in calendar.

2.3 Assign Jobs and Change Status

Technicians can assign jobs and update status conversationally.

Example prompts

  • Assign job of order 4001701 and operation 0010 to me
  • Start the job of order 4001701 and operation 0010

Assigning and updating maintenance jobs using SAP Joule in Perform Maintenance Jobs (F5104A).

2.4 Record Time on Jobs

Time confirmations can also be entered using natural language.

Example prompt

  • Record 1 hour for job of order 4001701 and operation 0010

Recording actual work time on a maintenance job using SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management.

2.5 View Materials and Services

Material and service visibility is available in calendar context.

Example prompt

  • Show materials for my jobs in a calendar

Material requirements for maintenance jobs displayed using SAP Joule.

Example prompt

  • Show services for maintenance jobs assigned to me in calendar

Service requirements for maintenance jobs displayed using SAP Joule.

3.      Create and Manage Maintenance Events

Joule also supports maintenance event planning, tightly integrated with planning buckets.

3.1 Create Maintenance Events

Users can create maintenance events using natural language.

Example prompt

  • Create a maintenance event for Compressor of type BI from 01/06/2026 at 9:00:00 until 15/06/2026 at17:00:00

Creating a maintenance event using SAP Joule with automatic planning bucket creation in S/4HANA 2025.

3.2 View Upcoming Maintenance Events

Upcoming events can be displayed with or without time filters.

Example prompt

  • Show upcoming maintenance events

Upcoming maintenance events displayed via SAP Joule with access to planning buckets.

3.3 View Maintenance Orders assigned to a Maintenance Event

Maintenance Orders assigned to a maintenance event can be displayed.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders and notifications that are assigned to a maintenance event 00000001

Displaying maintenance orders assigned to a Maintenance Event.

 

3.4 Assign Orders to Maintenance Events

Maintenance orders can be assigned to events conversationally.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders that can be assigned to maintenance event 00000001 --> Open Order

Viewing maintenance order details before assigning to a Maintenance Event.

Example prompt

  • Show maintenance orders that can be assigned to maintenance event 00000001 --> Add to Event

Assigning a maintenance order to a maintenance event using SAP Joule.

Example prompt

  • Assign order 4001721 to maintenance event 00000001

Assigning a specific maintenance order to a Maintenance Event.

Key Takeaways

  • SAP Joule brings conversational AI into transactional Asset Management.
  • Users can retrieve data, execute jobs, record time, and manage events using natural language.
  • Tight integration with SAP Fiori apps ensures governance and consistency.
  • S/4HANA 2025 marks a significant step towards AI‑assisted maintenance execution and planning.

References

  • SAP Help
  •  Maintenance Event Planning Agent

entry.title: Pick List Enhancement : Delivery Creation Based on Pick List in SAP Business One
entry.content:
The evolution of SAP Business One continues to bring powerful enhancements for developers.  With SAP Business One 10.0 FP 2508, a significant new interface was introduced in the DI API to support the creation of target documents from Pick Lists.

This enhancement provides a much-needed, direct method to link documents created from base documents (such as Sales Orders) to Pick List data. This is accomplished using two new properties:

SAPbobsCOM.Documents.Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry

SAPbobsCOM.Documents.Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum

But how does this new feature perform in the real world? In this blog, we will put it to the test and explore how this functionality behaves under four different inventory configurations and business scenarios. While this blog uses Delivery Notes as examples, the same principles apply to other supported target documents created using the DI API. Let's dive in.

Scenario 1 : Items are NOT managed by Batches / Serial Numbers and Warehouses are NOT allocated by Bin Locations

Case A : Behavior of Delivery creation from one Pick List among multiple Pick Lists for a single Sales Order

  1. Create a Sales Order with a quantity of 10.
  2. Generate three Pick Lists based on the Sales Order with the following quantities:
    • Pick List 1 → Quantity: 2
    • Pick List 2 → Quantity: 3
    • Pick List 3 → Quantity: 5
  3. From Pick List 2, pick a quantity of 3.
  4. Create a Delivery Note based on the Sales Order and Pick List 2 using the DI API code. For example:
SAPbobsCOM.Documents oDocuments = null;
SAPbobsCOM.Document_Lines oDocument_Lines = null;
oDocuments = (SAPbobsCOM.Documents)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oDeliveryNotes);
oDocument_Lines = oDocuments.Lines;
oDocuments.CardCode = "C20000";
oDocument_Lines.BaseEntry = 407;
oDocument_Lines.BaseLine = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BaseType = 17;
oDocument_Lines.Quantity = 3;

//Pick List Reference
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry = 57;     //PickList AbsEntry (AbsEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum = 0;   //PickList Line Number (PickEntry From PKL1)      
int RetVal = oDocuments.Add();

Result:

  • In the DLN1 table, the field PickIdNo is correctly updated with the correct Pick List AbsEntry.
  • Only Pick List 2 (the one used in the Delivery) is closed, while the other two Pick Lists remain open.

Scenario 2 : Items are managed by Batches / Serial Numbers and Warehouses are NOT allocated by Bin Locations

Case A : Behavior of Delivery Creation from a Pick List for a single-line Sales Order with full quantity allocated to a single batch

  1. Create a Sales Order with a quantity of 10, and assign the entire quantity to a single batch (for example: B11).
  2. Generate a Pick List based on the Sales Order and allocate all 10 quantity to the same batch (B11).
  3. Pick the entire quantity (10 quantity) from the Pick List.
  4. Create a Delivery Note based on the Sales Order and Pick List for the full picked quantity using the DI API code. For example:
SAPbobsCOM.Documents oDocuments = null;
SAPbobsCOM.Document_Lines oDocument_Lines = null;
oDocuments = (SAPbobsCOM.Documents)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oDeliveryNotes);
oDocument_Lines = oDocuments.Lines;
oDocuments.CardCode = "C20000";
oDocument_Lines.BaseEntry = 408;
oDocument_Lines.BaseLine = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BaseType = 17;
oDocument_Lines.Quantity = 10;

//Batch Allocation
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.BatchNumber = "B11"; 
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.Quantity = 10;

//Pick List Reference
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry = 59;    //PickList AbsEntry (AbsEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum = 0;  //PickList Line Number (PickEntry From PKL1
int RetVal = oDocuments.Add();​

Result:

  • In the DLN1 table, the field PickIdNo is correctly updated with the correct Pick List AbsEntry.
  • The Pick List is successfully closed after creating the Delivery.

Scenario 3 : Items are NOT managed by Batches / Serial Numbers and Warehouses are allocated by Bin Locations

Case A :  Behavior of Delivery creation from a Pick List for a single-line Sales Order with full quantity allocated to a single bin location

  1. Create a Sales Order with a quantity of 10 in a warehouse that is enabled for bin locations.

  2. Generate a Pick List based on the Sales Order and allocate the full quantity (10 quantity) to a single bin location.

  3. Pick the entire 10 quantity from the specified bin location.

  4. Create a Delivery Note for the picked quantity using the DI API. For example:
SAPbobsCOM.Documents oDocuments = null;
SAPbobsCOM.Document_Lines oDocument_Lines = null;
oDocuments = (SAPbobsCOM.Documents)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oDeliveryNotes);
oDocument_Lines = oDocuments.Lines;
oDocuments.CardCode = "C20000";
oDocument_Lines.BaseEntry = 388;
oDocument_Lines.BaseLine = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BaseType = 17;
oDocument_Lines.Quantity = 10;

//Bin Allocation
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BinAbsEntry = 2;  
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.Quantity = 10;

//Pick List Reference
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry = 36;      //PickList AbsEntry (AbsEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum = 0;    //PickList Line Number (PickEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListBatchAndBinLineNum = 0;   //PKL2 Line Number (Pkl2LinNum in PKL2 table)
int RetVal = oDocuments.Add();​

Result:

  • In the DLN1 table, the field PickIdNo is correctly updated with the corresponding Pick List AbsEntry.
  • The Pick List is successfully closed after the Delivery is created.

Case B :  Behavior of partial Delivery from a Pick List with quantity allocated across multiple bin locations

  1. Create a Sales Order with a total quantity of 10.
  2. Generate a Pick List based on the Sales Order and allocate quantities across two different bin locations:
    • Line 1 → Quantity: 4
    • Line 2 → Quantity: 6
  3. Perform partial picking as follows:
    • Pick 1 quantity from Line 1
    • Pick 2 quantities from Line 2
  4. Create a Delivery Note for the picked quantity (1 quantity  from Line 1) using the DI API. Example:
SAPbobsCOM.Documents oDocuments = null;
SAPbobsCOM.Document_Lines oDocument_Lines = null;
oDocuments = (SAPbobsCOM.Documents)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oDeliveryNotes);
oDocument_Lines = oDocuments.Lines;
oDocuments.CardCode = "C20000";
oDocument_Lines.BaseEntry = 399;
oDocument_Lines.BaseLine = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BaseType = 17;
oDocument_Lines.Quantity = 1;

//Bin Allocation
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BinAbsEntry = 2;  
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.Quantity = 1; 

//Pick List Reference
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry = 47;     //PickList AbsEntry (AbsEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum = 0;   //PickList Line Number (PickEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListBatchAndBinLineNum = 0;   //PKL2 Line Number (Pkl2LinNum in PKL2 table)
int RetVal = oDocuments.Add();​

Result:

The Pick List is partially updated:

  • The Line 2 remains unchanged, as it is not referenced in the Delivery document.
  • The SAP Business One client displays the Pick List with updated values, showing the partially delivered quantities.

Scenario 4 : Items are managed by Batches / Serial Numbers and Warehouses are allocated by Bin Locations

Case A : Behavior of partial Delivery from a Pick List with multiple batch and bin location allocations

  1. Create a Sales Order with a total quantity of 10, and allocate the quantity across two different batches.
  2. Generate a Pick List based on the Sales Order.
  3. Assign each batch quantity to different bin locations. For example:
    • Batch B1 → Assigned to one bin location
    • Batch B2 → Assigned to another bin location
  4. The Pick List is displayed in the SAP Business One client reflecting the batch and bin allocations.
  5. Perform partial picking as follows:
    • Pick 1 quantity from the 2nd line
    • Pick 1 quantity from the 3rd line

  6. Create a Delivery Note for the picked quantities (2 quantities in total) using the DI API. Example:
SAPbobsCOM.Documents oDocuments = null;
SAPbobsCOM.Document_Lines oDocument_Lines = null;
oDocuments = (SAPbobsCOM.Documents)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oDeliveryNotes);
oDocument_Lines = oDocuments.Lines;
oDocuments.CardCode = "C20000";
oDocument_Lines.BaseEntry = 403;
oDocument_Lines.BaseLine = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BaseType = 17;
oDocument_Lines.Quantity = 2;

//Batch Allocation
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.BatchNumber = "B1"; ;
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.Quantity = 1;
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.Add();
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.BatchNumber = "B2"; ;
oDocument_Lines.BatchNumbers.Quantity = 1;

//Bin Allocation
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BinAbsEntry = 26;  
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.Quantity = 1;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.SerialAndBatchNumbersBaseLine = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.Add();
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BaseLineNumber = 0;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.BinAbsEntry = 2;  
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.Quantity = 1;
oDocument_Lines.BinAllocations.SerialAndBatchNumbersBaseLine = 1;

//Pick List Reference
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry = 51;    //PickList AbsEntry (AbsEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum = 0;  //PickList Line Number (PickEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListBatchAndBinLineNum = 1;   //PKL2 Line Number (Pkl2LinNum in PKL2 table)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.Add();
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListEntry = 51;   //PickList AbsEntry (AbsEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListLineNum = 0; //PickList Line Number (PickEntry From PKL1)
oDocument_Lines.PickLists.PickListBatchAndBinLineNum = 2;   //PKL2 Line Number (Pkl2LinNum in PKL2 table)
int RetVal = oDocuments.Add();​

Result:

  • The remaining quantities in the Pick List stay open and unchanged.
  • The Pick List is partially updated after the Delivery is created.

Key Takeaways

The introduction of Pick List reference properties in SAP Business One 10.0 FP 2508 provides essential functionality for DI API developers. Here are the key takeaways:

  • Explicit Control: Developers can now programmatically link target documents (e.g., Delivery Notes) to specific Pick Lists using the PickListEntry and PickListLineNum properties.
  • Problem Solved: This resolves the previous system behavior where creating a target document via DI API would close all associated Pick Lists indiscriminately. This fix is reflected in the updates to SAP Notes 3444183 and 2252361.
  • Consistent Application: The logic demonstrated with Delivery Notes is consistently applied across various inventory scenarios and is applicable to all supported target documents created from Pick Lists.

With this enhancement, developers can build more robust and accurate fulfillment automations. For a comprehensive overview of related functionalities, refer to the blog Pick List in SAP Business One SDK.

entry.title: How to get clean JSON response from BEx Query OData service?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

We exposed a BEx Query as an OData service in SAP BW/BPC and the API is working.

However, the response contains a lot of unnecessary technical fields, metadata, and extra records that are not needed for our external integration. We only need a clean JSON response with specific business fields.

Current issues:

  • too many technical fields in response
  • unnecessary metadata returned
  • response structure is difficult for external systems to consume
  • filtering output fields is limited

We are currently testing through Postman and standard generated OData service.

What is the best practice to return only required fields from a BEx Query OData service?

entry.title: Re: FS10N in S4 Hana layout not displaying BKTXT
entry.content:
In order to display further fields in the line item report FBL3N, refer to the following SAP notes:

984305  - "Line item: Definition of special fields(T012S)"
215798  - "FBL*N: Special fields are not displayed"

First create special fields following customizing paths described in note 984305.
Run reports BALVBUFDEL as described in notes 215798. Check in SE16 if the field is filled in the table (ex. BKPF-BKTXT). If the field is empty, the issue is in the transaction which posted the document, there is no display issue.

The basic structure for displaying the items is RFPOSX. From this entry and the relevant entries in the table T021S, the system generates the structure RFPOSXEXT, which is used for the display. The special fields have the prefix "U_".

Please also refer to the above documentation on SAP Help link:https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/fiaccounting/3361880331.html

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud TM – Is it possible to disable Execution Tracking / ASR stages for Freight
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Transportation Management with scope items 6W2 / 6W3 / 6W4.

During Freight Order execution, the system enforces mandatory stage-based Execution Tracking (ASR), e.g.:

  • Check-in / Check-out
  • Truck departed
  • Ready for warehouse procesing

If these stages are not manually confirmed by users, the process cannot continue, which significantly complicates daily operations. Our business scenario does not require detailed execution or yard-related tracking, only basic Freight Order execution (GI/GR, settlement).

I found SAP documentation stating that Execution Tracking Relevance can be configured at Freight Order Type level:

SPRO → Transportation Management → Freight Order Management → Define Freight Order Types → Execution Tracking Relevance (e.g. “No Execution Tracking”)
Track & Trace

According to the documentation, selecting No Execution Tracking should disable manual execution reporting entirely.
However, in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud we cannot find this configuration at all (no SSCUI / no FO type setting).

  • Is Execution Tracking / ASR for Freight Orders mandatory by design in SAP Public Cloud TM?
  • Is there any supported way in Public Cloud to disable or auto-confirm execution stages?
  • Is this configuration only available in On-Prem / Private Cloud, but intentionally restricted in Public Cloud?
  • Are there any best practices or roadmap plans for simplifying FO execution without mandatory stages?

entry.title: Websession SAC _ Connectivity to BW Update
